comments: The first is the beginning of the show up to and including the introduction of the Genesis concert (fading out during the opening crowd noise).
The second begins with a fade up during the crowd noise and runs through to the end of the programme.

track_listing.track: ‘Fairies Wear Boots (LP-We Sold Our Soul For Rock 'N' Roll)' (NEMS)
‘Like A Rolling Stone (2x LP-Bob Dylan At Budokan)' (CBS)

transcript.text: Hi there, this is Tommy Vance. Welcome to the Friday Rock Show. And tonight's the night you've all been waiting for.
In just about 26 minutes time, we're going to rebroadcast 90 minutes of Genesis as they were recorded at Mebworth on the 24th of June 1978.
90 minutes of ace music, uninterrupted. But to start off tonight, here's Manfred Mann's Earth Band.
A fabulous way to start a program, I think, and I hope you think so as well.
That's Manfred Mann's Earth Band from the album which was called Watch,
the live version of the song that was written by Bob Dylan aeons ago called Mighty Quinn.
Even Bob Dylan is in print somewhere saying that he thoroughly approved
of the way that Manfred Mann handled his material.
This is Frankie Miller.
It's even got 1979 on the label.
That's Frankie Miller, his new single which is called When I'm Away From You.
Thank you very much indeed for all the very nice Christmas wishes
that you send both to myself, Thomas Vance,
and also to Tony Wilson.
I'd like to just mention a few names.
Penny Reynolds up there in Birmingham.
Thank you, Penny.
Another little Christmas card with an owl on the front.
Sent by Heather Crosby and boyfriend,
Leakin Staffordshire, who is standing by for Genesis.
You won't have to stand by much longer.
Also a card from Eunice Skinner.
And finally, one from Kate and Morris Kitchen.
Thank you very much indeed.
We had a little card from Alan Roy.
Nine Douglas Roy in Inverness,
who wants to hear this.
A heavy band by the name of Black Sabbath first came out on the album Paranoid,
and we played it, as I said, specially for Alan Roy, 9 Douglas Row in Inverness.
Also, we played it from a double album, which is a really good compilation of the material put out by Black Sabbath.
Good band. Nice to see them on top of the pops earlier this year.
The compilation album is called We Sold Our Soul for Rock and Roll.
And here is a gentleman who's, no, I suppose, brought rock and roll up to date, Elvis Costello.
we talk strangers who talk money take the greasy foreign money skin sharp red leather hotline
be prepared for the engaged side rival books engagement rings
Standing in just like the slacks
Through future pocket
Back supports and picture lock
Deeper towns and sleeper trains
Through the darts and down the drains
Major roads and ladies' malls
Hearts of oak and long trunk calls
I'm the man of your dreams
Just like the Sunday's best
From your shy severed head
About the private lives, the swipes
Bob Costello in the tracks The Attractions from the LP Armed Forces,
which is just out.
You'll hear more of that on this programme next year.
The album is called...
What is it called? Armed Forces, that's right.
I get very mixed up about that album because of the cover.
It's the most complex cover I've ever seen in my life.
Here's Bob Dylan.
and like a rolling stone which is a track from the album which is called live at buddha khan
we were speaking to cbs sometime just before christmas and they said that this album is going
to be released sometime in 1979 if you want it at the moment it'll cost you in import style about 16
pounds plus it was the 24th of june in 1978 when this was recorded genesis 90 minutes uninterrupted
from nebworth very sad this song it's all about growing old growing old and the passing of time
time which waits for no man it's from a trick of the tail and it's called ripples back in 72
this next one's all about the very first that don't know what a hermaphrodite is i shall
a hermaphrodite is someone what is male and female at the same time in the same pair of trousers
this is what a hermaphrodite is now you don't see too many hermaphrodites around we have a few in
the road crew and i see a few in the audience but you don't see many old and this is the story of
the very first one it's called the fountain of salmasis one was all about american gold rush
i don't know if there's any americans in the audience tonight yes there's four of you
well we're going to do a song about the american gold rush which is of course when everybody packed
up their bags got in their cupboard wagons and went west west or west i'm not sure where west
is but it's over there somewhere this is called deep in the mother load issue somebody he's been
with us for the last three or four months and tonight first time you're seeing him good mate
of ours from milwaukee would you say hello to on the guitar and bass mr daryl sturm meanwhile on
with the show and we're going to sing a song plus little ditty from windham wuthering this is called
one for the vine it might not work but we're going to have a try every time i do this i'm going to
have a try this one's called the lady lies it was screamed from below he thinks he is a warrior
So he picks up his sword and goes
Off with the monster
He rescues the maiden fair
Ah, but we know she's a demon
Lure him to the demon's lair
Through his last voyage
To all trees he leads
To a house
Entering with her
We're going to be doing a tour of England next year
That's the last song, thanks a lot
Genesis as they performed at Nebworth
The 24th of June 1978
And first heard on Radio 1
July the 1st, 1978, in the Saturday show
hosted by Alan Freeman.
Big Al, wherever you are, Happy New Year.
Engineers on the Genesis recording
were Bill Aitken and Anthony Pugh.
What a performance.
Next week on the
Friday Rock Show, we'll have the Friday Night
Connection, loads of classic tracks and
sessions by Renaissance and Lena Lovitch.
Tony Wilson is our producer.
Our engineer tonight was Trevor. Thank you, Trevor.
Just time for me to say
from myself, Tommy Vance, and from Tony Wilson,
may 1979 be the most memorable in your history.
See you next week. Good night.
